summary:
The Commonwealth of Independent States (CIS) was established on December 8, 1991, when the leaders of Russia, Ukraine, and Belarus signed the Belavezha Accords declaring the Soviet Union dissolved and creating a new loose association in its place. Eight additional former Soviet republics joined through the Alma-Ata Declaration on December 21, bringing total membership to eleven. The Soviet flag over the Kremlin was lowered for the last time on December 25, 1991, when Gorbachev resigned.

The CIS was designed to manage the practical challenges of the Soviet Union's dissolution — coordinating military assets, dividing the Soviet nuclear arsenal, maintaining economic links, and handling the status of Russian-speaking minorities in the newly independent states. In practice, the organization's effectiveness was limited and uneven. Member states pursued divergent paths: the Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania) did not join at all and oriented themselves toward Western Europe and NATO; Ukraine and Georgia later distanced themselves from Russian-dominated structures. The CIS's most significant practical role lay in the coordination of border security, customs arrangements, and military basing rights. Russia retained control of the Soviet nuclear weapons on Ukrainian, Kazakh, and Belarusian territory, which were subsequently transferred to Russia under the Budapest Memorandum of 1994.
